front: Novel: the protagonist hopes to receive the title wound to make up for fleeing from the Battle of Chancellorsville
back: The Red Badge of Courage (Stephen Crane)

front: Novel: Jim Conklin and 'the tattered man' are companions of the protagonist, a soldier in the 304th New York Infantry Regiment
back: The Red Badge of Courage (Stephen Crane)

front: Novel: George Wyndham praised this work for viewing events through the protagonist's 'morbidly sensitive' temperament
back: The Red Badge of Courage (Stephen Crane)

front: Poem: opens with 'Black riders came from the sea' with 'clang and clang of spear and shield' and 'clash and clash of hoof and heel'
back: The Black Riders and Other Lines (Stephen Crane)
work: The Black Riders and Other Lines

front: Poem: a 'creature, naked, bestial' eats his own heart 'because it is bitter / And because it is my heart'
back: In the Desert, from The Black Riders (Stephen Crane)
work: The Black Riders and Other Lines

front: Poem: a spirit declares 'Ah, there is no God!' and is smote dead by 'a swift hand / a sword from the sky'
back: The Black Riders and Other Lines (Stephen Crane)
work: The Black Riders and Other Lines

front: Poem: a man tells the universe 'Sir, I exist!' but is told 'The fact has not created within me a sense of obligation'
back: War Is Kind (Stephen Crane)

front: Poem: a seer shows the speaker 'the book of wisdom,' after which the speaker notes 'strange that I should have grown so suddenly blind'
back: The Black Riders and Other Lines (Stephen Crane)
work: The Black Riders and Other Lines

front: Poem: a man sees a ball made of clay when he possesses it but made of gold when it is in the sky
back: The Black Riders and Other Lines (Stephen Crane)
work: The Black Riders and Other Lines

front: Poem: an 'ashamed' figure who says 'if I did good deeds, I know not of them' is praised as the 'best little blade of grass' by God
back: The Black Riders and Other Lines (Stephen Crane)
work: The Black Riders and Other Lines

front: Poem: ironically instructs 'Do not weep, maiden' and describes how 'these men were born to drill and die'
back: War Is Kind (Stephen Crane)

front: Novel: the protagonist, a shirt factory worker, becomes a prostitute after being dumped by the bartender Pete, who is seduced by Nellie in the 'hilarious hall'
back: Maggie: A Girl of the Streets (Stephen Crane)
work: Maggie: A Girl of the Streets

front: Novel: ends with the protagonist's mother screaming 'I'll fergive her!' at the funeral; Jimmie Johnson fights boys from Devil's Row in the opening
back: Maggie: A Girl of the Streets (Stephen Crane)
work: Maggie: A Girl of the Streets

front: Short Story: opens with 'None of them knew the color of the sky'; inspired by the author's shipwreck on the SS Commodore
back: The Open Boat (Stephen Crane)

front: Short Story: four men adrift in a dinghy repeatedly murmur 'Funny they don't see us'; the oiler (Billie) dies trying to swim to shore
back: The Open Boat (Stephen Crane)

front: Short Story: two characters argue about whether 'houses of refuge' are staffed; the correspondent recites 'A soldier of the Legion lay dying in Algiers'
back: The Open Boat (Stephen Crane)

front: Short Story: the Swede accuses Johnnie of cheating at cards during a blizzard; ends with a cowboy asking 'I didn't do anything, did I?'
back: The Blue Hotel (Stephen Crane)

front: Short Story: a character says another 'isn't even a noun. He is kind of an adverb' and claims 'Every sin is the result of a collaboration'
back: The Blue Hotel (Stephen Crane)

front: Short Story: Scratchy Wilson lowers his revolvers in disbelief when he discovers the marshal Jack Potter has returned with a new bride and is unarmed
back: The Bride Comes to Yellow Sky (Stephen Crane)
work: The Bride Comes to Yellow Sky

front: Short Story: the black coachman Henry Johnson's face is disfigured by a 'red snakelike' chemical during a house fire while saving a boy; set in Whilomville
back: The Monster (Stephen Crane)

front: Short Story: the girl Sadie Winter is scared at a birthday party by a disfigured figure at the window; the Trescott family is ostracized for sheltering the victim
back: The Monster (Stephen Crane)

front: Short Story: a young man drinks free soup at a bar and lodges with an assassin after disguising himself as a homeless man
back: An Experiment in Misery (Stephen Crane)

front: Short Story: ends with a child's drunk father fatally throwing a dog out a window as an allegory for Jim Crow
back: A Dark Brown Dog (Stephen Crane)
